message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 infectiousness might differ by infection location. Nevertheless, no such study of infectiousness has been reported.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Object</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The study objective was estimation of the reproduction number by infection location.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Patients who infected no one were ignored because their reliability might be lower than that of patients who infected more than one person. On the assumption that the histogram follows an exponential distribution, we estimated the reproduction number from the histogram of the number of people infected by the same patient.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Night entertainment venues showed the greatest infectiousness, followed by facilities for elderly people and hospitals. Nursery schools and workplaces were followed by homes, with the lowest infectiousness.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Discussion and Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Countermeasures under the second declaration of emergency status targeted restaurants. However, infectiousness at restaurants was not high. Comparable to those of universities and karaoke, and not significantly different from homes: the least infectious location studied.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Estimating SARS-CoV-2 reproduction number by infection location in Japan

reference.unstructured: Sugishita Y , Kurita J , Sugawara T , Ohkusa Y. .Effects of voluntary event cancellation and school closure as countermeasures against COVID-19 outbreak in Japan. Plos one 2020. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0239455

H Nishiura , H Oshitani , T Kobayashi , T Saito , T Sunagawa , T Wakita , MHLW COVID-19 Response Team. Closed environments facilitate secondary transmission of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). doi:https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.02.28.20029272

Kurita J , Hata T , Sugawara T , Ohkusa Y , Hata A. .An Estimation of Reproduction Number of SARS-CoV-2 by Age Class for Age Classes in Japan. https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2021.01.14.21249854v1.full
Japan Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare. Press Releases. https://www.mhlw.go.jp/stf/newpage_10723.html (in Japanese) [accessed on Deember 10, 2020]
